question: Prove that you can use dominoes to tile a rectangular checkerboard with an even number of squares.
response: A clean way to prove this uses a simple coloring argument.  Imagine any rectangular checkerboard with an even number of squares (say an (m \times n) board where (mn) is even). Color it in the usual alternating pattern like a chessboard: black and white.  ### Key observations:  1. **Equal number of black and white squares**    Because the total number of squares is even, the board has exactly the same number of black squares as white squares.  2. **Each domino covers one black and one white square**    A domino always covers two adjacent squares. On a checkerboard, adjacent squares are always opposite colors—one black and one white.  ---  ### Construction (tiling method)  Now we show you can actually tile it:  * If one side of the rectangle is even (say (m) is even), then each row has an even number of squares. * You can tile each row by placing dominoes horizontally, covering pairs of adjacent squares. * This works row by row until the whole board is covered.  Alternatively:  * If (n) is even, you can tile column by column using vertical dominoes.  ---  ### Why this always works  Since (mn) is even, **at least one of (m) or (n) must be even**. That guarantees one of the above constructions works.  ---  ### Conclusion  Every rectangular checkerboard with an even number of squares can be completely tiled by dominoes, because:  * The parity ensures a perfect pairing exists, and * A simple row-wise or column-wise construction always achieves it.
